20 Fifth Avenue | "The Berkeley Rise"


MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS

[Verse 1]


[40] "Twenty Fifth Avenue, southwest corner Ninth" → Inspired by: "The Berkeley, southwest corner of

9th Street." Brown. (1924) Fifth Avenue Old and New, p. 36.

[39] "Berkeley on the block since eighteen-seventy-nine" → Drawn from the 1879 Appleton's Dictionary listing: "BERKELEY, 5th av. and 9th st." Appleton. (1879) Appleton's Dictionary of New York, p. 6.

[3] "Stone front six stories, brick with basement laid" → Pulled from the 1897 Bromley's Atlas: "1

Structure; 6 Story Brick building with Basement and Stone front." Bromley. 1897 Bromleys Atlas, p. 16.

[39] "Rents from eight thousand down to five hundred paid" → Drawn from: "The rents of these

apartments range from $8,000 to $500 a year." Appleton. (1879) Appleton's Dictionary of New York, p. 6.

[40] "Grace Church interwoven with the life of New York" → Directly from: "For more than a century,

Grace Church has been interwoven with the life of New York." Brown. (1924) Fifth Avenue Old and New,

p. 36.

[40] "Historic fabric of Fifth Avenue, where the legends talk" → Drawn from: "its history and ideals are

part of the historic fabric of Fifth Avenue." Brown. (1924) Fifth Avenue Old and New, p. 36.

[Chorus]


[5] "Seventeen stories, steel and fireproof frame" → From the 1955 Manhattan Land Book: "17 Story Brick or Stone or Concrete building with Basement and Elevators and Fire proof building (steel construction)." Bromley. 1955 Manhattan Land Book, p. 32.

[13] "Built in nineteen-forty, they replaced the name" → Directly from: "Was built in 1940." Unknown

Author. Landmarks Preservation Commission, p. 1.

[38] "From hotel to tower, watch the skyline grow" → Inspired by: "17-Story Apartment House Nearing Completion on Berkeley Hotel Site." Stern. (1987) New York 1930, p. 798.

[38] "Boak and Paris drew it — now the whole world know" → From: "Other notable buildings by Boak

& Paris include...20 Fifth Avenue." Stern. (1987) New York 1930, p. 798.

[Verse 2]


[2] "Eighteen-fifty-three, not a structure stood" → From: "In the 1853 Perris Maps, on 20 Fifth Avenue,

there is reference to 0 Structures." Perris. 1853 Perris Maps, p. 62.

[1] "Eighteen-sixty-seven, still an empty hood" → From: "In the 1867 Dripps Plan, on 20 Fifth Avenue,

there is reference to 0 Structures." Dripps. 1867 Dripps Plan, p. 7.

[4] "By eighteen-eighty-five, Robinson wrote 'Berkley' — stone" → From: "In the 1885 Robinsons Atlas, on 20 Fifth Avenue, there is written 'BERKLEY' as well as reference to 1 Structure; Stone building." Robinson. 1885 Robinsons Atlas, p. 12.

[3] "Eighteen-ninety-seven, Bromley mapped it grown" → From: "In the 1897 Bromleys Atlas, on 20 Fifth Avenue, there is written 'Berkeley' as well as reference to 1 Structure; 6 Story Brick building with Basement and Stone front." Bromley. 1897 Bromleys Atlas, p. 16.

[12] "Educators, lawyers, surgeons called it home" → Inspired by Ralph Edgar Pickett's entry: "Home:

20 Fifth Av., N.Y.C. 11." Marquis. (1961) Who's Who in the East Eighth Edition, p. 714.

[12] "Pickett, Freedman, Haines — Fifth Avenue's throne" (Pickett) → From: "Home: 20 Fifth Av.,

N.Y.C. 11." Marquis. (1961) Who's Who in the East Eighth Edition, p. 714.

[25] "Pickett, Freedman, Haines — Fifth Avenue's throne" (Freedman) → From: "Home: 20 Fifth Av.,

N.Y.C. 11." Marquis. (1959) Who's Who in the East Seventh Edition, p. 317.

[8] "Pickett, Freedman, Haines — Fifth Avenue's throne" (Haines) → From: "Haines, John Peter: born New York City, December 17th, 1851; Humanitarian." Brainard. (1911) Who's Who In New York City and State, p. 418.

[Bridge]

[19] "The Row on Washington Square, Wharton wrote the scene" → From: "Edith Wharton, William

Dean Howells, and Henry James, all lived and worked at No. 1." White & Willensky. (1968) AIA Edition 1,

p. 66.

[19] "Dos Passos penned Manhattan Transfer at thirteen" → From: "John Dos Passos wrote

'Manhattan Transfer' in No. 3." White & Willensky. (1968) AIA Edition 1, p. 66.

[19] "No. 2 Fifth Avenue brought the outcry loud" → From: "when the huge No. 2 Fifth Avenue

apartment tower was being planned, citizens put up an outcry." White & Willensky. (1968) AIA Edition 1,

p. 66.

[5] "But Twenty Fifth still stands, unbowed, unbowed" → Anchored in the enduring 17-story structure noted in the 1955 Manhattan Land Book: "17 Story Brick or Stone or Concrete building with Basement and Elevators and Fire proof building (steel construction)." Bromley. 1955 Manhattan Land Book, p. 32.

[Outro]


[5] "From zero structures to seventeen floors of steel" → From: "17 Story Brick or Stone or Concrete building with Basement and Elevators and Fire proof building (steel construction)." Bromley. 1955 Manhattan Land Book, p. 32.

[13] "Twenty Fifth Avenue — forever real" → Anchored in the building's documented construction: "Was

built in 1940." Unknown Author. Landmarks Preservation Commission, p. 1.
